edges.description: Loss of ATP13A2 polyamine export causes polyamine accumulation in lysosomes.
edges.source: ATP13A2 loss impairs lysosomal polyamine transport

edges.description: Primary polyamine storage disrupts GCase/BMP interactions and impairs lysosomal substrate hydrolysis.
Lysosomal dysfunction converges with impaired mitochondrial maintenance and clearance
intermediate_mechanisms: Impaired lysosomal substrate hydrolysis and autophagy-lysosome stress

edges.description: Impaired GCase-mediated hydrolysis is accompanied by secondary accumulation of its substrate glucosylsphingosine.
Lysosomal hydrolase dysfunction converges on Parkinson-relevant neurodegenerative pathways.
Mitochondrial dysfunction and impaired mitochondrial clearance are downstream mechanisms contributing to the progressive neurodegenerative phenotype.
Degeneration of dopaminergic motor circuitry produces young-onset parkinsonism.
Corticospinal or pyramidal involvement contributes to lower-extremity spastic paraparesis.
Neurodegenerative motor-system involvement can include abnormal ocular motility.
Facial nerve-innervated muscle myokymia is part of the KRS motor phenotype.
Progressive KRS neurodegeneration includes cognitive decline.
Neuropsychiatric complications can arise in the progressive KRS syndrome.
Increased lysosomal polyamine signal reports the primary storage event caused by ATP13A2 loss.
Increased glucosylsphingosine tracks the secondary lysosomal substrate storage branch downstream of impaired GCase activity.

edges.target: ATP13A2 loss impairs lysosomal polyamine transport

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome/PARK9: One Novel and One Possible Recurring Ashkenazi ATP13A2 Mutation.
evidence.snippet: KRS is caused by homozygous/compound heterozygous inactivating mutations in ATP13A2.
ATP13A2 | HGNC:30213 | Kufor-Rakeb syndrome | MONDO:0011706 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: ATP13A2 is a lysosomal P5B-ATPase that transports polyamines. In Kufor-Rakeb syndrome, loss-of-function ATP13A2 variants impair endolysosomal polyamine transport, producing lysosomal dysfunction and creating a mechanistic basis for neurodegeneration.
nodes.id: ATP13A2 loss impairs lysosomal polyamine transport

evidence.reference_title: Structural basis of polyamine transport by human ATP13A2 (PARK9).
evidence.snippet: Recent studies have suggested that ATP13A2 and its close homologs, collectively known as P5B-ATPases, are polyamine transporters at endo-/lysosomes.

evidence.reference_title: Lysosomal polyamine storage upon ATP13A2 loss impairs β-glucocerebrosidase via altered lysosomal pH and electrostatic hydrolase-lipid interactions.
evidence.snippet: Here, we demonstrate lysosomal polyamine accumulation in ATP13A2 knockout (KO) cell lines and human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neurons.

nodes.description: Abnormal ocular motility is part of the characteristic Kufor-Rakeb syndrome presentation; some clinical descriptions specify supranuclear gaze abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome/PARK9: One Novel and One Possible Recurring Ashkenazi ATP13A2 Mutation.
evidence.snippet: Kufor-Rakeb syndrome (KRS)/PARK9 presents with autosomal recessive young onset Parkinson's disease (YOPD), spastic paraparesis, abnormal eye movements and facial myokymia.

nodes.description: Progressive cognitive decline is part of the neurodegenerative phenotype of Kufor-Rakeb syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome in a Guatemalan Patient With an ATP13A2 Gene Pathogenic Variant: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: Here, we present the first documented Guatemalan case of KRS, a young woman with progressive motor and cognitive decline.

nodes.description: Fine involuntary contractions of facial nerve-innervated muscles are listed among the canonical Kufor-Rakeb syndrome motor features.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome/PARK9: One Novel and One Possible Recurring Ashkenazi ATP13A2 Mutation.
evidence.snippet: Kufor-Rakeb syndrome (KRS)/PARK9 presents with autosomal recessive young onset Parkinson's disease (YOPD), spastic paraparesis, abnormal eye movements and facial myokymia.

evidence.reference_title: Lysosomal polyamine storage upon ATP13A2 loss impairs β-glucocerebrosidase via altered lysosomal pH and electrostatic hydrolase-lipid interactions.
evidence.snippet: Primary polyamine storage caused secondary storage of lysosomal anionic phospholipid bis(monoacylglycero)phosphate (BMP) and an age-dependent increase in the β-glucocerebrosidase (GCase) substrate glucosylsphingosine in Atp13a2 KO brains.

readouts.interpretation: Increased glucosylsphingosine tracks the secondary lysosomal substrate storage branch downstream of impaired GCase activity.

nodes.description: ATP13A2 deficiency causes lysosomal polyamine accumulation that disrupts lysosomal pH and impairs β-glucocerebrosidase-mediated substrate hydrolysis.

evidence.reference_title: Lysosomal polyamine storage upon ATP13A2 loss impairs β-glucocerebrosidase via altered lysosomal pH and electrostatic hydrolase-lipid interactions.
evidence.snippet: Therefore, excess polyamine compromises lysosomes by disrupting pH and electrostatic interactions between GCase and BMP that enable efficient substrate hydrolysis, potentially clarifying pathogenic mechanisms and suggesting convergence on PD-relevant pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Lysosomal polyamine storage upon ATP13A2 loss impairs β-glucocerebrosidase via altered lysosomal pH and electrostatic hydrolase-lipid interactions.
evidence.snippet: Here, we demonstrate lysosomal polyamine accumulation in ATP13A2 knockout (KO) cell lines and human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neurons.

readouts.interpretation: Increased lysosomal polyamine signal reports the primary storage event caused by ATP13A2 loss.

nodes.description: ATP13A2 loss produces primary lysosomal storage of polyamines in cell lines and human iPSC-derived neurons. This primary storage perturbs lysosomal pH and electrostatic lipid-hydrolase interactions, setting up secondary lysosomal substrate storage.

evidence.reference_title: Lysosomal polyamine storage upon ATP13A2 loss impairs β-glucocerebrosidase via altered lysosomal pH and electrostatic hydrolase-lipid interactions.
evidence.snippet: Here, we demonstrate lysosomal polyamine accumulation in ATP13A2 knockout (KO) cell lines and human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neurons.

nodes.description: ATP13A2 deficiency impairs maintenance of a healthy mitochondrial pool and likely compromises mitochondrial clearance, contributing to progressive neuronal dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: ATP13A2 mutations impair mitochondrial function in fibroblasts from patients with Kufor-Rakeb syndrome.
evidence.snippet: These findings collectively suggest that ATP13A2 contributes to the maintenance of a healthy mitochondrial pool, supporting the hypothesis that impaired mitochondrial clearance represents an important pathogenic mechanism underlying KRS.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome in a Guatemalan Patient With an ATP13A2 Gene Pathogenic Variant: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: This P5B-ATPase dysfunction impairs lysosomal processing, leading to the accumulation of α-synuclein.

nodes.description: Juvenile- or young-onset parkinsonism is the defining motor syndrome of Kufor-Rakeb disease.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome/PARK9: One Novel and One Possible Recurring Ashkenazi ATP13A2 Mutation.
evidence.snippet: Kufor-Rakeb syndrome (KRS)/PARK9 presents with autosomal recessive young onset Parkinson's disease (YOPD), spastic paraparesis, abnormal eye movements and facial myokymia.

nodes.description: Low-dose aripiprazole can be used to control psychotic symptoms in Kufor-Rakeb syndrome without major motor worsening in at least isolated reported cases.

evidence.reference_title: Successful treatment of psychosis in a patient with Kufor-Rakeb syndrome with low dose aripiprazole: a case report.
evidence.snippet: This significant improvement without drug-induced motor side effects suggests that aripiprazole at low doses (2-5 mg) is effective and tolerated in patients with KRS.

nodes.description: KRS is an ATP13A2-related juvenile parkinsonism syndrome in which lysosomal and mitochondrial dysfunction converge on progressive neuronal injury, particularly affecting dopaminergic motor circuitry and broader cognitive and neuropsychiatric function.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome in a Guatemalan Patient With an ATP13A2 Gene Pathogenic Variant: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: Here, we present the first documented Guatemalan case of KRS, a young woman with progressive motor and cognitive decline.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome in a Guatemalan Patient With an ATP13A2 Gene Pathogenic Variant: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative condition characterized by progressive loss of dopaminergic neurons and by heterogeneous etiologies and clinical manifestations.

nodes.description: Psychosis can complicate Kufor-Rakeb syndrome in some patients.

evidence.reference_title: Successful treatment of psychosis in a patient with Kufor-Rakeb syndrome with low dose aripiprazole: a case report.
evidence.snippet: We present a case of a 32-year-old male with Kufor-Rakeb syndrome (KRS), a form of juvenile parkinsonism due to mutations of the ATP13A2 gene at PARK9 locus. The patient was seen for daily behavioral outbursts and psychotic symptoms.

nodes.description: Pyramidal involvement with lower-extremity spastic paraparesis is a classic accompanying motor feature.

evidence.reference_title: Kufor-Rakeb Syndrome/PARK9: One Novel and One Possible Recurring Ashkenazi ATP13A2 Mutation.
evidence.snippet: Kufor-Rakeb syndrome (KRS)/PARK9 presents with autosomal recessive young onset Parkinson's disease (YOPD), spastic paraparesis, abnormal eye movements and facial myokymia.
